planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 10–12; Ivory Cane Palm is a graceful, clustering palm native to the rainforests of Java, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula — one of the most ornamental of the tropical understory palms, producing multiple slender, ivory-white to yellowish-green canes topped by elegant, pinnate fronds with characteristic jaggered, fishtail-tipped leaflets — excellent for shaded tropical garden positions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Pinanga coronata is adapted to rainforest understory conditions and should be protected from direct sun, especially harsh afternoon sun, and from cold below 45°F — mulch deeply and maintain consistent moisture. Pinanga coronata (Ivory Cane Palm or Crowned Pinanga) is a multiple-stemmed, clumping palm in the large Pinanga genus native to the lowland and sub-montane rainforests of Java, Bali, Sumatra, and Peninsular Malaysia, where it is a common and characteristic element of the forest understory. The palm is distinguished by its attractive, ivory-white to pale-yellowish-green cane-like stems — slender (1–1.5 inch diameter), smooth, prominently ringed — arising in clusters to create an elegant, bamboo-like multi-stemmed effect topped by graceful, arching pinnate fronds with attractively jagged (praemorse) leaflet tips. The Pinanga genus contains hundreds of species from South and Southeast Asian rainforests, most of which share the characteristic of being shade-adapted understory palms growing beneath the closed canopy. Pinanga coronata is among the most widely cultivated Pinanga for tropical gardens in zone 10–12, valued for its manageable size, elegant multi-stemmed habit, and tolerance of deep shade. Small red to black fruits appear periodically in colorful clusters. An outstanding shade-tolerant, multi-stemmed palm for tropical garden understory positions.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Pinanga coronata
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Pinanga collection

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in zone 9–11; Satake Palm is a rare, monotypic palm endemic to the Yaeyama Islands of the Ryukyu Archipelago (southernmost Japan), growing in humid subtropical forest — a graceful, solitary-trunked feather palm with a prominently colored, distinctive reddish-brown crown shaft and elegantly arching fronds, one of the rarest palms in cultivation.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Satakentia liukiuensis grows moderately fast for a palm and is reasonably adaptable to subtropical conditions with regular watering and palm fertilizer twice yearly — protect from cold below 26°F. Satakentia liukiuensis (Satake Palm or Ryukyu Palm) is a monotypic genus — a single species known only from the wild in the Yaeyama Island group (Iriomote, Ishigaki) of the Ryukyu Archipelago in southernmost Japan, where it grows in humid subtropical evergreen forest. Named in honor of the Japanese botanist Yoshitami Satake who first described it, the Satake Palm is known to only a small number of palm enthusiasts worldwide. The palm develops a moderately sized, solitary trunk reaching 30–40 feet in maturity, with a distinctive, prominently colored reddish-brown to coppery-red crown shaft — the smooth, enclosed basal section of the fronds just below the crown — that is one of the palm's most ornamental features. The gracefully arching, deep-green feather fronds create an elegant, formal crown. Satakentia is adaptable to subtropical conditions in zones 9–11 and grows reasonably well in South Florida, the Gulf Coast, and California. A genuinely rare and prized collector's palm that is slowly becoming more available through specialized nurseries.
sources: Japanese Ministry of Environment — Yaeyama Endemic Plants

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in deep shade in zone 10–12; Calyptrate Schismatoglottis is a compact, clumping, forest-floor aroid native to the humid tropical rainforests of Borneo, Peninsular Malaysia, and Sumatra — one of 100+ Schismatoglottis species — producing compact rosettes of small, attractive, mottled or plain, glossy leaves adapted to extremely low light conditions in deep forest understory.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic, consistently moist but well-drained soil in deep to moderate shade; Schismatoglottis calyptrata requires very high humidity (above 70%), warmth, and low-light conditions mimicking rainforest floor environments — excellent for terrariums, vivaria, and tropical shade garden floors. Schismatoglottis calyptrata (Calyptrate Schismatoglottis) is a compact, clumping, rhizomatous aroid in the Araceae family native to the forest floor of the humid tropical rainforests of Borneo, Sumatra, and Peninsular Malaysia — one of over 100 described Schismatoglottis species, a genus endemic primarily to the Sunda region (Borneo, Sumatra, Malay Peninsula, Philippines). The plant forms dense, low rosettes of small (4–8 inch), broadly ovate, somewhat glossy leaves on short petioles, varying from plain green to subtly patterned with silver or pale mottling depending on population. In its natural habitat, Schismatoglottis species grow on the permanently moist, leaf-litter-covered forest floor in deep shade — one of the lowest-light plant niches in the humid tropics. This deep-shade adaptation makes Schismatoglottis calyptrata an effective, low-growing groundcover for the darkest positions in tropical and subtropical gardens, terrarium collections, and vivarium designs. The genus has recently gained attention in the rare plant collector community; many Schismatoglottis species show extraordinary leaf patterning and diversity.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Schismatoglottis calyptrata
Kew Monograph of Schismatoglottis — Hay and Yuzammi (2000)

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to deep shade in zone 10–12; Blushing Philodendron is a vigorous, climbing, epiphytic aroid native to the Andean foothills and lowland forests of Colombia and Ecuador, the wild parent of many popular cultivated Philodendron varieties, bearing glossy, dark-green, heart-shaped leaves with distinctive red-maroon undersides and red-flushed petioles — producing long aerial-rooting stems that climb tree trunks and walls.

directions: Plant or root cuttings in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade with a support — moss pole, tree trunk, or trellis — for climbing; Philodendron erubescens attaches by aerial roots and climbs vigorously in warm, humid conditions; water moderately and maintain high humidity for best growth. Philodendron erubescens (Blushing Philodendron or Red-leaf Philodendron) is a vigorous, hemi-epiphytic, climbing aroid native to the rainforests and cloud-forest edges of Colombia, Ecuador, and adjacent South America — the wild parent species from which many of the most popular commercial Philodendron cultivars were developed, including 'Imperial Red,' 'Painted Lady,' and 'Pink Princess.' In its wild form, P. erubescens produces large, glossy, arrow or heart-shaped leaves (up to 12–16 inches long) with deep-green upper surfaces and distinctive, attractive red-maroon to copper-red undersides — the 'blushing' coloration of the leaf undersides and the reddish-pink to red coloration of new leaves and petioles gives the species its name. The plant climbs vigorously by aerial roots that cling to rough bark, moss poles, or textured surfaces. In tropical outdoor cultivation (zone 10–12), P. erubescens makes an excellent climber on tree trunks, walls, and large trellises, providing attractive, large-leaved coverage in partial to deep shade positions. Contains calcium oxalate crystals — toxic if ingested by humans or pets.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew Monograph — Philodendron

planting.note: Plant slips (rooted cuttings from sprouted tubers) 2–3 weeks after last frost in full sun in loose, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in zone 8–12; Purple Sweet Potato varieties (Okinawan, Japanese, Stokes) produce deeply purple-fleshed tubers with exceptional levels of anthocyanin antioxidants — 2–3 times higher than blueberries — distinct from the orange-flesh sweet potatoes, with a slightly drier, nuttier, less sweet flavor profile valued in Asian-American cooking and superfood markets.

directions: Plant slips 2–3 weeks after last frost in loose, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Purple Sweet Potato produces better tubers in slightly poor, loose soil rather than heavily amended rich soil — excess nitrogen encourages vine growth at the expense of tuber development; harvest 90–120 days after transplanting when the vines begin to yellow. Ipomoea batatas 'Purple' (Purple Sweet Potato, Okinawan Sweet Potato, or Stokes Purple Sweet Potato) encompasses a group of purple-fleshed sweet potato varieties — including 'Okinawan,' 'Japanese Purple' (Satsumaimo), and 'Stokes Purple' — sharing the characteristic of producing tubers with deep violet-purple flesh caused by exceptionally high concentrations of anthocyanin pigments (cyanidin and peonidin glycosides). These anthocyanins give purple sweet potatoes one of the highest antioxidant levels of any commonly eaten vegetable — ORAC values 2–3 times higher than blueberries — and have generated significant interest in functional food and nutraceutical research. Purple-fleshed sweet potatoes retain their vibrant purple color when baked or boiled (better than in acidic cooking), are somewhat drier and nuttier in texture than orange sweet potatoes, and are traditional in Okinawan Japanese cuisine (where they are called 'beni imo'), Hawaiian cuisine (Okinawan sweet potato poi), Filipino cuisine, and increasingly in American superfood markets. Grow exactly like standard sweet potato: loose, sandy soil, full sun, long warm season. Slips (not tubers or seeds) are the standard planting material.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Sweet Potato Production
Okinawan Foods Research — Purple Sweet Potato Anthocyanins
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Sweet Potato Varieties

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–11; Baronii Palm is a graceful, clustering, shade-adapted palm endemic to the rainforests of eastern and northwestern Madagascar — producing multiple slender canes topped by elegant arching fronds — one of the more cold-tolerant Dypsis species from Madagascar's highlands, performing well in zone 9 with protection.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Dypsis baronii is a clustering palm that produces new stems (suckers) from the base — more drought tolerant than many Dypsis species due to its highland Madagascar origin; water moderately and apply palm fertilizer twice yearly. Dypsis baronii (Baronii Palm or Madagascar Bamboo Palm) is a multi-stemmed, clustering palm endemic to Madagascar — one of the most palm-diverse countries on Earth, with over 170 endemic palm species — native to the humid forests of eastern and northwestern Madagascar at moderate elevations. The genus Dypsis (formerly split into Chrysalidocarpus and several other genera) is the largest palm genus in Madagascar. Dypsis baronii produces multiple slender, bamboo-like stems (1–2 inch diameter) in dense clusters, topped by graceful, arching, pinnate fronds with slightly arching, feathery leaflets — creating an elegant, clumping effect. Among the more cold-hardy Dypsis species available, it has performed in zone 9b with some protection in California and Gulf Coast gardens. The clustering habit and moderate size make it suitable for smaller gardens than the more commonly planted Areca Palm (Dypsis lutescens). Excellent as a privacy screen, garden accent, or container specimen in zone 9–11 subtropical gardens.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew Madagascar Palm Project
